reviews.summary: The paper scales Memory Mosaics—an architecture based on associative memories that replaces attention with key–value kernel retrieval—up to ~10B parameters (“v2”) and evaluates it on large-scale, real-world data (1T tokens). It introduces three architectural changes (adaptive kernel bandwidth, a gated time-variant key extractor, and a three-level memory with short- and long-term components) and proposes a three-pronged evaluation protocol: persistent-knowledge storage (standard LM benchmarks), new-knowledge storage (long-context, multi-document QA), and in-context learning (few-shot classification). The main claim is that Memory Mosaics v2 matches similarly sized transformers on persistent knowledge tasks while substantially outperforming them on new-knowledge storage and in-context learning, even when transformers are trained on up to 8× more data.
strengths: Introduces an adaptive bandwidth schedule for Gaussian-kernel associative memory linked to memory size (β = β1 n^α + β0), a principled idea grounded in kernel regression bandwidth selection.
Proposes a gated time-variant key extractor that semantically modulates recurrence weights, plausibly improving content-based temporal aggregation without position encodings.

Splits contextual memory into short-term and long-term associative memories with stochastic long-term masking during training, yielding a clear inductive bias for positional vs position-invariant signals and improved length extrapolation.
Offers a clean, probabilistic perspective on attention-as-kernel-regression and leverages symmetric keys/queries, contributing to interpretability.
Evaluates across three dimensions (persistent vs new knowledge vs in-context learning), reducing common confounds when assessing “learning new tasks.”
Provides ablations indicating that removing long-term memory does not hurt a subset of standard benchmarks but degrades multi-doc QA, supporting the intended role of long-term memory.
Explores data scaling for transformers (200B, 1T, 8T tokens) to test whether “more data” closes the gap, and provides negative results on anonymous-label ICL.
Reports fine-tuning speed comparisons showing rapid adaptation of Memory Mosaics v2.
Articulates the design choices and their motivations (e.g., induction-head analysis, exchangeability of associative memory).
Provides training details, hyperparameters, and descriptions of masking strategies; includes diagnostic plots (attention patterns, length distributions).
Addresses a central open problem—robust in-context learning and long-context reasoning—at LLM scales with real datasets.

If validated, the empirical findings suggest a promising alternative to standard transformers for long-context and on-the-fly task learning, with practical benefits for adaptation and fine-tuning efficiency.
weaknesses: Computational complexity of long-term associative memory at 32k (and beyond) is not quantified with wall-clock/runtime or throughput; it is unclear how inference scales and whether it is practically competitive without additional sparsification.

The use of “values” that include a smoothed near-future signal (v_t depending on x_{t+1}) is unconventional; while not necessarily leaking information in causal decoding, the operational constraints and impact on training/inference dynamics require more explicit justification.
Stability and numerical behavior of the gated, exponential parameterization (e.g., g_T = exp(W_g x_T)) are not discussed; risk of exploding magnitudes vs normalization is not analyzed.

Long-context extrapolation baselines for transformers omit widely used RoPE extrapolation methods (e.g., position interpolation, scaling/NTK-aware/YaRN/LongRoPE variants), which can substantially improve extrapolation without retraining; the “×” entries suggest no attempt to evaluate such techniques, weakening fairness.

The transformer augmentation with “long-short attention” is reported as ineffective, but implementation specifics (masking, parameter tying, head allocation, training schedule) are not detailed; it is hard to assess whether this is a principled negative result or an under-optimized variant.

New-knowledge storage evaluation forces the question at the end; while justified to avoid prompt engineering, this design may unintentionally penalize baselines that are compatible with reading the question first (a common real usage pattern). A controlled comparison with both orders (and clear discussion of the trade-offs) would be informative.
RULER results at 4k show modest margins (1–6%), and statistical significance is not reported; confidence intervals or multiple seeds are missing throughout.

The classification ICL setup is prompt-sensitive; although appendix mentions some prompt variations, there is limited reporting on variance across seeds/templates and no standardized ICL harness comparison.

The “persistent vs new knowledge” attribution based on removing long-term memory is interesting but somewhat coarse; some tasks might partially rely on both. A finer-grained analysis would strengthen the causal interpretation.
The exact parameterization, head allocation, and cross-layer interactions of short- vs long-term memories are only sketched; more architectural specifics would aid reproducibility.
Limited discussion of retrieval-augmented baselines (e.g., RETRO-style or external-memory variants) that could be highly competitive on multi-doc QA when the question appears last.

Kernelized or linear-attention variants (Performer, cosFormer), Hopfield networks/modern Hopfield memory, KNN-LM, and slot-attention-like methods are not thoroughly compared or discussed as alternative associative memory implementations.
Recent long-context transformer improvements (position scaling/interpolation, hybrid attentions, sparse block attention, LongNet/MoBA) are not included as baselines.
reviews.questions:
1. What are the exact time/memory costs (wall-clock, tokens/sec, peak memory) of Memory Mosaics v2 vs transformers at 4k, 32k, and 64k during both training and inference? Please include numbers for the long- and short-term memory components separately.
2. How sensitive are the results to the adaptive bandwidth schedule (β1, β0, α)? Could you provide ablations that disable or vary this schedule, and separate ablations for each of the three architectural changes (adaptive bandwidth, gated keys, three-level memory)?
3. For fairness, did you evaluate transformer baselines with RoPE extrapolation techniques (e.g., position interpolation, scaling/NTK-aware/YaRN/LongRoPE) or stronger long-context training curricula? If yes, please report; if not, can you discuss expected impact?
4. Can you clarify the construction of values that incorporate x_{t+1}? During inference on a prefix, are values computed only from observed tokens (i < current t), and how do you avoid any information leak when generating x_{t+1}?
5. How robust are the ICL results to prompt variations, ordering, and different delimiters across multiple random seeds? Could you provide confidence intervals or standard deviations, and possibly results using standardized ICL harnesses?
6. Why exclude retrieval-augmented transformer baselines on the multi-document QA tasks, given they are designed to store and retrieve relevant information? Would including a RETRO-style or modern RAG baseline change conclusions?
7. The “transformer + long/short attention” augmentation underperformed. Could you detail the architectural choices (masking, head allocation, parameter budgets, training schedule) and provide training curves to rule out under-optimization?
8. Do you plan to release pretrained models and training/inference code, including the long-context kernels, masking strategies, and memory implementations? If partial release, what components will be available?
reviews.assessment: This paper presents a compelling scaled study of an associative-memory-based alternative to transformers and provides credible evidence that the proposed Memory Mosaics v2 matches transformers on standard LM-style benchmarks while offering notable advantages on long-context new-knowledge storage and in-context learning. The architectural innovations are well-motivated, the three-dimension evaluation helps isolate capabilities, and several ablations substantiate the intended roles of the components. However, the work would be substantially strengthened by: (i) more rigorous and fair transformer baselines for length extrapolation and long-context usage (e.g., modern RoPE scaling/interpolation and retrieval-augmented models); (ii) clear computational profiling and scaling characteristics of the associative memories at long contexts; (iii) deeper ablations (including each new component in isolation) and statistical reporting. Overall, I find the direction promising and the results encouraging enough to merit publication, contingent on clarifying fairness and adding key empirical details. I recommend acceptance with minor-to-moderate revisions.
